summary: Justin Brennan walks through underwriting a 28-unit, 1956-built Class C apartment complex in Texas pre-market deal, analyzing physical components, market positioning, financial projections, and key risk factors like chiller systems and plumbing infrastructure with Gupta who toured the property.
takeaways: Inspect all plumbing laterals with cameras down every line—budget $3-5K for a 26-unit for full sewer line inspection; use findings as leverage against seller for price reduction or credit
Run expense ratio at minimum 10-15% vacancy/credit loss for properties 1980 or older in GA/TN/AL markets (45-55% total operating expense ratio); only drop to 5-7% in tight coastal markets
Verify copper wiring not aluminum—pull receptacles to confirm; aluminum creates fire risk and insurance cost increases

Chiller systems are deal-killers if single unit serves all 28 apartments; if it fails in summer heat, property becomes uninhabitable; avoid or require backup parts on-site and trained maintenance staff
Check utility bills for last 24 months in detail; don't rely on broker estimates—this is where most underwriting errors hide on older buildings
Aerial inspection: property should be surrounded by single-family homes and other apartments, NOT commercial/industrial; drive streets counting window bars—any bars indicate problem neighborhood

Pre-market deals from reputable brokers (TMG, MMG) come with pre-market advantage; use 25-question seller disclosure form as addendum before executing contract to create leverage for issues found during due diligence

On $2.2M deal with $67K equity piece at 10% down: acquisition fee of 2-3% ($45-67K) plus $22K earnest money plus $5-8K inspections = fully covers initial capital requirement, then reimburse from closing costs

skill_candidates.change: Systematically verify utility bills, expense assumptions, and run expense ratios at 10-15% minimum vacancy for older properties instead of accepting broker projections
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin showed how broker numbers (32% utilities, 3% vacancy) were severely understated; emphasized 'run it at a minimum 10%' and '10 to 20%' for properties 1980+ in GA/TN/AL; stated 'There's no way on planet Earth you're operating at 32%'

skill_candidates.change: Require full plumbing line camera inspection with leverage mechanism: use sewer findings to negotiate seller credit/price reduction rather than accepting property as-is
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin spent 15+ minutes on plumbing risks, stated 'you'll know very quickly where there's backups, where there's issues, where there's tree roots' and 'that becomes a negotiation item against the seller to either reduce price or ask for a big credit'

skill_candidates.change: Identify and price-adjust for deal-killer systems (chiller vs individual HVAC units) before making offer; treat 1956-era chiller failures as uninhabitable risk requiring premium equity buffer
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin: 'if that chiller goes out on all 28 units, you're going to have a show up your ass' and 'you'll be praying to baby Lord Jesus' and 'if you have to replace a chiller, get ready...that's a capex problem'; emphasized avoiding chiller systems 'at all cost'

skill_candidates.change: Conduct aerial and street-view inspection: verify single-family residential surroundings, count window bars for neighborhood safety signal, confirm no liquor stores/smoke shops nearby before touring
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin showed Google Maps aerial and street view, asked 'does anybody see anything?' and when identifying single-family homes said 'one thing you really like to see...is that your property is surrounded by single family homes'; identified 'bars on the windows' as red flag for neighborhood quality

title: Weekly Coaching_06-05-25  | Justin Brennan
